Step 4 — Identify limits: The claim that the sage is fully eudaimon regardless of physical circumstances may be psychologically unrealistic. Chronic pain genuinely changes what activities are available. Aristotle's counter-point (some circumstances genuinely impair flourishing) may need acknowledgment. The framework says little about practical and political dimensions of managing illness.

Step 5 — Adapt: Use the Stoic dichotomy of control to clarify focus; supplement with Aristotle's acknowledgment that some circumstances genuinely matter; add Confucian relational attention to the people whose support is essential. The result is a more realistic, multi-framework approach.
workedExamples.conclusion: The worked application demonstrates that faithful application is richer than wholesale adoption: you use what the Stoic framework genuinely offers (the dichotomy of control, the reframing of what is genuinely good), acknowledge where it reaches its limits (the unrealistic sage ideal, the practical dimensions of illness management), and fill the gaps with other frameworks. This is philosophy in action, not philosophy as doctrine.

retrievalPractice.prompt: State the five features of the application method. For each, describe in one sentence what it requires.

A person is dealing with persistent anxiety about an upcoming high-stakes job interview. Using the framework selection guide, identify which ancient tradition is most relevant, explain what it specifically says about this type of situation, and identify one limit of applying it here.
What is the difference between wholesale adoption and selective adaptation when applying an ancient philosophical framework? Give one example of each.

Identify one specific limit of applying classical Confucianism to contemporary decisions about family obligations in a Western context. Explain what historical assumption the limit reflects, and describe how the core Confucian insight could be adapted while acknowledging the limit.

transferExercises.scenario: This is the culminating exercise of the course. Choose a genuine live question or challenge in your own life — something you are genuinely uncertain about or struggling with.
transferExercises.task: Apply the five-feature application method in full: (1) identify which ancient framework(s) are most relevant and justify your selection; (2) apply the framework faithfully and specifically to your situation — not a vague paraphrase but the actual doctrine applied; (3) identify at least two specific limits of the framework in your context and explain what historical assumptions those limits reflect; (4) describe a justified selective adaptation, potentially drawing on a second tradition to supplement the first; (5) reflect on what philosophical engagement added — what it helped you see that you could not see before.
successCriteria: Framework selection is justified with reference to the type of problem, not personal preference alone
Application is specific — the actual doctrine is applied, not a vague paraphrase
At least two specific limits are identified with explanation of what historical assumptions they reflect
The selective adaptation is justified, not arbitrary — the learner explains why they combined or modified the frameworks as they did
The final reflection demonstrates genuine philosophical thinking: the learner articulates something they now see that they could not see before engaging with the philosophy

assessments.title: Section 1 Quiz: What Was Ancient Philosophy?

questions.prompt:
According to the definition used in this course, what is philosophy's primary tool for examining foundational questions?
A) Narrative and storytelling
B) Observation and controlled experiment
C) Argument and reason
D) Revelation and sacred authority
successCriteria: Selects C
Can explain why argument distinguishes philosophy from myth, religion, and science

rubric.excellent: Selects C and articulates that philosophy's distinctive move is demanding reasons that can be assessed by any rational person, distinguishing it from narrative (myth), authority (religion), and empirical observation (science).
rubric.adequate: Selects C without full articulation of why, or partially explains the distinction.
rubric.weak: Selects any other option, or confuses philosophy's method with another domain's primary tool.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects B (observation and experiment), treating philosophy as equivalent to empirical science — reflecting a dismissal of non-empirical inquiry as primitive or derivative.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the domain comparison table in found-definition content block and ask the learner to identify what philosophy's method uniquely contributes that science does not.

questions.prompt:
The term 'Axial Age' refers to:
A) The period when ancient empires began using axles for transportation, enabling trade
B) The roughly simultaneous emergence of sophisticated philosophical traditions across Greece, India, and China, approximately 800–200 BCE
C) The Roman imperial period during which Greek philosophy spread westward
D) The period when ancient texts were first translated into Latin
successCriteria: Selects B
Can describe Karl Jaspers' hypothesis about why this convergence occurred

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains Jaspers' hypothesis: the parallel emergence was driven by the breakdown of Bronze Age mythic authority, the spread of literacy, pluralism, and crisis across all three regions simultaneously.
rubric.adequate: Selects B with minimal elaboration.
rubric.weak: Selects any other option; confuses the Axial Age with a specific empire or translation movement.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects C or D, reflecting the misconception that philosophical development was primarily a Western/Roman story with no parallel developments in India or China.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the found-geography content block and the Axial Age section; ask the learner to list specific thinkers from each region with dates showing contemporaneity.

questions.prompt:
Which of the following is the BEST example of philosophical inquiry, as distinguished from scientific or religious inquiry?
A) Conducting experiments to determine how fast objects fall
B) Examining whether 'justice' can be given a coherent definition that covers all cases we call just
C) Consulting sacred texts to determine how to perform a ritual correctly
D) Writing a narrative poem about the origin of the gods
successCriteria: Selects B
Explains that B involves conceptual analysis of foundational assumptions rather than empirical observation or appeal to authority

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that examining the concept of justice is philosophical because it targets a foundational assumption (what 'justice' means), uses argument and reason, and is self-critical rather than empirical or revelatory.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without articulating why it is distinctively philosophical.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C, or D, conflating philosophy with science, religion, or literature.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D (narrative poem about gods), treating literary or mythic engagement with foundational questions as equivalent to philosophical argument.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the domain comparison table in found-definition and ask learner to identify the specific method used in each domain and why method, not subject matter, is the distinguishing criterion.

questions.prompt:
When the lecture says that 'translation is never neutral,' which does it mean?
A) Ancient translators were untrustworthy and made intentional errors
B) Key philosophical terms carry multiple layers of meaning that no single English word captures, making translation an act of interpretation
C) You should always read ancient philosophy in the original language
D) Modern translations of ancient texts are always politically biased
successCriteria: Selects B
Can give a specific example using logos, dharma, or dào

rubric.excellent: Selects B and gives a specific example: e.g., that translating logos as simply 'reason' collapses the term's range across 'word,' 'rational principle,' 'speech,' and 'argument,' each of which has different philosophical implications.
rubric.adequate: Selects B but gives only a generic explanation without a specific example.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C, or D, treating the translation problem as a matter of bad faith, requirement for original-language competence, or political bias.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects C, concluding that the solution to translation complexity is reading in original languages — missing the philosophical point that the interpretive problem is constitutive of reading ancient philosophy, not solved by language competence alone.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the found-translation content block; ask the learner to consider what three different English translations of the same passage would emphasize differently and why.

questions.prompt:
According to the five-step reading method, what should you do FIRST when approaching a primary philosophical text?
A) Find a famous quotable line to represent the text's main idea
B) Look up what scholars have written about the text
C) Identify the genre and form of the text
D) Memorize the historical context in which it was written
successCriteria: Selects C
Can explain why genre and form determine what interpretive strategy is appropriate

rubric.excellent: Selects C and explains that genre (dialogue, aphorism, sutra, treatise) determines how arguments are encoded and what kind of interpretation is required — a Platonic dialogue uses dramatic irony; a Stoic maxim needs unpacking.
rubric.adequate: Selects C without explaining why genre is the first step.
rubric.weak: Selects A (quotemining approach), B (secondary sources before primary engagement), or D (memorizing context before reading the text itself).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — reflecting the misconception that philosophical texts are collections of quotable maxims rather than arguments requiring structural analysis.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Walk through the Heraclitus worked example (we-found-heraclitus) and show how the quotable line is the conclusion of a multi-step argument — the aphoristic compression makes sense only when the genre is identified first.

questions.prompt: In 2–3 sentences, explain why the claim 'ancient ideas arose in isolation' is historically false. Give at least one specific example.
successCriteria: States that trade routes, conquest, and migration produced documented contact
Gives at least one specific example (e.g., Milindapañha, Buddhist missionaries in Alexandria, Alexander's conquests)
Does not confuse contact with sameness — contact is compatible with genuine philosophical differences

rubric.excellent: States the claim is false, cites a specific documented contact (e.g., the Milindapañha recording dialogue between Greek king Menander I and Buddhist monk Nāgasena; or Buddhist missionaries reaching Alexandria in the 3rd century BCE; or Alexander's conquests bringing Greek culture into contact with Persian, Egyptian, and Indian thought), and notes that contact makes the differences between traditions more interesting, not less.
rubric.adequate: States the claim is false and gives a vague reference to trade routes or cultural contact without a specific example.
rubric.weak: States the claim is false without providing any supporting evidence, or provides an inaccurate example.

errorPatterns.description: Learner fails to provide a specific example, or states that contact did not produce philosophical exchange — reflecting the hermetically sealed box misconception.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the found-conditions content block, specifically the paragraph on cross-pollination, and ask the learner to identify three documented contact events and what philosophical exchange each produced.

questions.prompt: Using the worked example of Heraclitus in the lecture, write out the argument structure (premises and conclusion) of his river fragment in your own words. Do not copy the lecture text.
successCriteria: Identifies at least two distinct premises
Correctly identifies the conclusion as a claim about the nature of identity (not just 'everything changes')
Uses own words — does not copy the lecture

rubric.excellent: Identifies Premise 1 (the water in a river constantly flows, so the water you step in the first time is not the water you step in a second time), Premise 2 (you yourself change continuously in body, memory, and mental state), and Conclusion (what we call 'the same river' or 'the same person' is a label applied to a constantly changing process — identity is not a fixed fact but a functional label). Paraphrases in own words.
rubric.adequate: Identifies two premises but states the conclusion as 'everything changes' without specifying the claim about identity.
rubric.weak: Quotes the lecture directly, or identifies only one premise, or misconstrues the conclusion as a claim about meaninglessness rather than identity.

errorPatterns.description: Learner quotes the lecture text instead of reconstructing the argument in their own words — demonstrating surface reading rather than genuine argument comprehension.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Ask the learner to close the lecture and describe the Heraclitus argument from memory; then compare their reconstruction to the text to identify what they retained and what slipped.

assessments.title: Section 2 Quiz: Greek and Hellenistic Traditions

questions.prompt:
Aristotle says that virtue is a 'mean' (meson) between deficiency and excess. Which of the following is the BEST example of this doctrine applied to honesty?
A) An honest person tells every truth they know at every opportunity
B) An honest person avoids lying but also avoids brutal bluntness that causes unnecessary harm
C) An honest person always tells the truth because truth has intrinsic value regardless of consequences
D) An honest person tells the truth only when it benefits them
successCriteria: Selects B
Identifies that B describes the mean between two extremes — dishonesty/deception (deficiency) and brutal/indiscriminate candor (excess)

rubric.excellent: Selects B and identifies the deficiency (excessive flattery or deception) and excess (brutal indiscriminate bluntness) that B avoids — demonstrating that the mean is not mathematical but context-sensitive.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without identifying both extremes.
rubric.weak: Selects A (excess — indiscriminate truth-telling), C (conflating virtue with a Kantian rule), or D (instrumentalizing honesty).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A, reflecting a maximizing interpretation of virtue (always do the maximum) rather than the Aristotelian mean — treating virtue as the extreme rather than the balanced disposition.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the virtue ethics section in exp-greek-lecture; ask the learner to identify the deficiency and excess for three virtues (courage, generosity, honesty) to consolidate the mean doctrine.

questions.prompt:
Epictetus says: 'Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are, and you will have a tranquil flow of life.' Which of the following responses BEST captures what an Aristotelian critic would say about this advice?
A) Epictetus is correct — the key to happiness is accepting all circumstances as they are
B) Epictetus is correct, but he should add that virtue requires performing good actions
C) Epictetus's advice makes happiness fully independent of external circumstances, but this fails to account for the fact that some misfortunes genuinely impair the full exercise of our capacities
D) Epictetus is wrong because he ignores the role of the gods in human affairs
successCriteria: Selects C
Can articulate the Aristotelian position: eudaimonia requires both virtue and sufficient external goods; catastrophic misfortune genuinely impairs flourishing

rubric.excellent: Selects C and articulates that Aristotle's position is that eudaimonia is an activity requiring external goods and circumstances — not merely an inner state — and that some misfortunes (chronic illness, loss of all relationships, grinding poverty) genuinely impair flourishing regardless of character.
rubric.adequate: Selects C but states the Aristotelian position only vaguely.
rubric.weak: Selects A (agreeing with Stoicism), B (missing the core disagreement), or D (introducing theological considerations not present in the Aristotelian critique).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A, equating Stoic acceptance with a correct response rather than identifying the philosophical debate — possibly because they have not distinguished Stoic and Aristotelian positions on the role of external goods.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the worked debate in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to reconstruct both Position A (Stoic) and Position B (Aristotelian) from memory, focusing on where they agree and where they genuinely diverge.

questions.prompt:
Which of the following BEST describes what the Stoics meant by 'eupatheiai' (healthy emotions)?
A) Emotions that have been suppressed through willpower and discipline
B) Emotions that arise from accurate beliefs about what is genuinely valuable, as opposed to passions that arise from false beliefs
C) Emotions experienced by the Stoic sage only after achieving perfect wisdom
D) Emotions associated with physical health and bodily well-being
successCriteria: Selects B
Can give at least one example of a eupatheia contrasted with the passion it replaces

rubric.excellent: Selects B and gives a concrete example — e.g., caution (a eupatheia: rational concern about things worth caring about) vs. fear (a passion: based on falsely valuing external goods); or joy (based on accurate appreciation of genuine goods) vs. pleasure (based on attachment to preferred indifferents).
rubric.adequate: Selects B without a concrete example.
rubric.weak: Selects A (the suppression misconception), C (treating eupatheiai as exclusively available to sages), or D (conflating emotional health with physical health).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — demonstrating the core Stoic misconception that the goal is emotional suppression rather than emotional accuracy.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the Enchiridion worked example (we-greek-epictetus) and the eupatheiai section of exp-greek-lecture; ask the learner to identify three eupatheiai and explain why each reflects accurate belief rather than suppressed feeling.

questions.prompt:
Plato's Allegory of the Cave uses the image of prisoners seeing shadows to illustrate a philosophical point about knowledge. What is the MAIN philosophical point the allegory makes?
A) Knowledge is unreliable because our senses can deceive us
B) Most people are imprisoned by social conventions that prevent them from thinking freely
C) Genuine knowledge requires turning away from the sensory world toward the abstract Forms; sense experience yields only belief, not knowledge
D) Philosophers should not enter politics because their vision is too different from ordinary people
successCriteria: Selects C
Can articulate the epistemic distinction between doxa (belief from sensory experience) and episteme (knowledge of the Forms)

rubric.excellent: Selects C and explains that the cave allegory illustrates Plato's two-world epistemology: physical things are imperfect shadows of the Forms; sense perception yields only doxa (belief about shadows); genuine episteme (knowledge) requires intellectual ascent to the Forms — culminating in knowledge of the Form of the Good (the sun).
rubric.adequate: Selects C but conflates the epistemic point with a general claim about skepticism.
rubric.weak: Selects A (general skepticism about senses without the specific Platonic two-world theory), B (social commentary), or D (political implication without the epistemological foundation).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D, focusing on the political implication (philosopher-kings) without the epistemological foundation — reading the cave allegory instrumentally rather than philosophically.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Plato section in exp-greek-lecture and ask the learner to reconstruct the argument structure: what must be true about knowledge (epistemology) for Plato's political conclusion (philosopher-kings) to follow?

questions.prompt:
Epicurus famously argued: 'When I am, death is not; when death is, I am not.' What philosophical point is this meant to establish?
A) Death is inevitable and we should spend our lives preparing for it
B) There is no subject present to experience death, so death cannot be an evil for the person who dies
C) The soul is immortal and therefore death is not truly the end of experience
D) We should avoid thinking about death to maintain mental tranquility
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain that this is the symmetry argument: just as non-existence before birth was not bad for us, non-existence after death will not be bad for us

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains the symmetry argument: non-existence before birth was not experienced as bad; non-existence after death will not be experienced as bad for the same reason — there is no subject present to suffer the deprivation.
rubric.adequate: Selects B but does not articulate the symmetry argument.
rubric.weak: Selects A (general mortality acknowledgment), C (immortality claim that directly contradicts Epicurean materialism), or D (avoidance strategy that misses the positive philosophical argument).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects C (soul immortality), demonstrating the conflation of Epicureanism with Platonism or popular afterlife belief — missing that Epicurus was an atomist materialist who denied the afterlife.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Epicureanism section in exp-greek-lecture; ask the learner to identify what Epicurus's materialist atomism (from Democritus) implies about the soul and death, and how the tetrapharmakos item on death follows from this.

questions.prompt: Reconstruct the Stoic argument for why virtue is the only genuine good. Your answer should include at least two premises and a conclusion, and use the criterion of 'unconditional benefit' to evaluate external goods.
successCriteria: States a criterion for 'genuine good' (unconditional benefit in all circumstances for all people)
Applies the criterion to external goods and finds them lacking
States that virtue meets the criterion
Draws the conclusion that only virtue is genuinely good

rubric.excellent: Reconstructs: Premise 1 — a genuine good is something that benefits its possessor unconditionally, in all circumstances, for all people. Premise 2 — external goods (health, wealth, reputation) fail this test: they can be used for harmful ends, they can be taken away, and people have flourished without them. Premise 3 — virtue (the stable, rational disposition to act and feel rightly) benefits unconditionally and cannot be taken away against the virtuous person's will. Conclusion — only virtue is a genuine good.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the criterion and applies it to at least one external good and to virtue, but misses one premise or states the conclusion imprecisely.
rubric.weak: Lists Stoic claims without reconstructing the argument structure, or confuses the Stoic position with Aristotle's.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats the Stoic claim as 'just their opinion' rather than a structured argument with premises that can be evaluated for their truth and the inference for its validity.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to exp-greek-argument (the case study) and ask the learner to identify what the Stoics need to be true about the definition of 'good' for their argument to work — making the evaluable premise explicit.

questions.prompt: Aristotle criticizes Plato's Theory of Forms with the 'Third Man Argument.' In your own words: (a) what is the problem Plato's Theory of Forms was trying to solve? (b) what is the Third Man Argument? (c) how does Aristotle's own theory of forms (immanent in particular things) avoid this problem?
successCriteria: Correctly states Plato's problem (how do we have knowledge of perfect equality, beauty, etc. given that physical things are imperfect?)
Correctly states the Third Man Argument (positing a Form to explain the relationship between things and their Form just generates a need for another Form to explain that relationship, and so on)
Correctly states Aristotle's solution (forms are real but exist in particular things, not in a separate realm — when the horse dies, the form perishes)

rubric.excellent: Answers all three parts accurately and in own words: (a) Plato's problem is explaining how we have clear concepts of perfect equality/beauty/goodness when no physical thing is perfectly equal/beautiful/good — positing separate, eternal Forms as the source of our concepts. (b) The Third Man Argument: if the Form of Horse explains what makes individual horses horses, you need another Form to explain the relationship between individual horses and the Form of Horse — and so on infinitely. (c) Aristotle's solution: forms exist in particular things, not separately — the form of a horse is in each horse, and when the horse dies the form perishes too.
rubric.adequate: Answers two of three parts accurately.
rubric.weak: Answers only one part accurately, or conflates Plato's and Aristotle's positions.

errorPatterns.description: Learner describes the Third Man Argument as simply 'Aristotle disagrees with Plato' without reconstructing the specific logical problem the argument identifies — treating philosophical disagreement as assertion rather than argument.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Aristotle section in exp-greek-lecture; ask the learner to identify the exact logical move the Third Man Argument makes — what assumption of Plato's does it exploit, and how does Aristotle's immanent forms avoid triggering the same regress?

questions.prompt:
Which of the following correctly distinguishes āstika from nāstika schools in Indian philosophy?
A) Āstika schools are theistic; nāstika schools are atheistic
B) Āstika schools accept the authority of the Vedas; nāstika schools reject it
C) Āstika schools are older; nāstika schools are later developments
D) Āstika schools are philosophical; nāstika schools are purely religious
successCriteria: Selects B
Can give one example of each — e.g., Nyāya (āstika) and Buddhism (nāstika)

rubric.excellent: Selects B and names at least one āstika school (e.g., Nyāya, Sāṃkhya, Vedānta) and one nāstika school (e.g., Buddhism, Jainism, Cārvāka), noting that the distinction is about stance toward Vedic authority, not about theism or chronology.
rubric.adequate: Selects B with minimal elaboration.
rubric.weak: Selects A (confuses the distinction with theism vs. atheism — plausible but imprecise), C, or D.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D — conflating the nāstika schools (particularly Buddhism and Jainism) with religion rather than philosophy, reflecting the misconception that Indian philosophy is not rigorous philosophical inquiry.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Indian landscape section in exp-indian-lecture; ask the learner to identify two specifically philosophical (not just religious) contributions of a nāstika school — e.g., the Buddhist anattā argument or Cārvāka's critique of inference.

questions.prompt:
The Buddhist doctrine of anattā (no-self) most directly contradicts which of the following?
A) The Sāṃkhya doctrine that Puruṣa is a permanent witness-consciousness
B) The Cārvāka doctrine that the self is identical to the body
C) The Upanishadic doctrine that ātman is identical to Brahman
D) The Nyāya doctrine that perception is a valid source of knowledge
successCriteria: Selects C
Can explain that anattā is a direct rejection of the Upanishadic permanent, divine self — not just any account of selfhood

rubric.excellent: Selects C and explains that the Buddha's anattā doctrine was a direct response to the Upanishadic claim that behind all experience is an unchanging witness-consciousness (ātman) identical to Brahman. The Buddhist argument is that no aggregate of the person (form, sensation, perception, mental formations, consciousness) can be identified as this permanent, controlling self.
rubric.adequate: Selects C but explains the contradiction only vaguely.
rubric.weak: Selects A (Sāṃkhya also posits a permanent witness, but the historical target of anattā is specifically the Upanishadic ātman doctrine); B (Cārvāka's body-self is a materialism that the Buddha also rejects, but anattā most directly contradicts the Upanishadic doctrine); or D (irrelevant to the self-doctrine).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A or B — confusing the target of the anattā doctrine with Sāṃkhya's permanent Puruṣa or Cārvāka's body-self, rather than identifying the Upanishadic ātman as the direct philosophical target.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the anattā worked example (we-indian-anatta) and the Upanishadic section in exp-indian-lecture; ask the learner to identify what specific claim the Buddha's argument is designed to refute — tracing the argument to its exact philosophical target.

questions.prompt:
In the Nyāya five-member inference, the udāharaṇa (example) step serves which philosophical purpose?
A) It provides an emotional appeal that makes the argument more persuasive
B) It grounds the universal pervasion (vyāpti) claim in a verified observed instance
C) It defines the technical terms used in the inference
D) It shows that a similar inference has been accepted by reliable authorities
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain that without the example, the vyāpti claim is an unverified universal — exactly what Cārvāka criticizes

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that the example (e.g., 'wherever there is smoke, there is fire — as in a kitchen') grounds the universal pervasion claim in an observed instance where the connection has been directly verified. Without this grounding, the inference would be making an unverified universal claim — precisely what Cārvāka attacks.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without connecting to the Cārvāka critique.
rubric.weak: Selects A (rhetorical appeal), C (definition), or D (appeal to authority — Nyāya grounds inference in perception, not authority).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D — treating the Nyāya inference as relying on authority rather than on the empirical grounding of vyāpti in observed cases, reflecting a failure to appreciate the rigor of Indian epistemological methodology.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Nyāya section in exp-indian-lecture and the Indian text analysis interaction; ask the learner to trace the epistemological justification for each step of the five-member inference and identify which step does the empirical work.

questions.prompt:
Cārvāka philosophy argues that inference is not a truly reliable source of knowledge. Which of the following BEST represents the Cārvāka argument?
A) Inference is unreliable because human reasoning is often motivated by self-interest
B) Inference is unreliable because it depends on universal connections that can only be established by exhaustive observation — which we cannot achieve
C) Inference is unreliable because language is always ambiguous and premises cannot be stated precisely
D) Inference is unreliable because only direct mystical experience gives access to truth
successCriteria: Selects B
Can note that this argument anticipates Hume's problem of induction by over a thousand years

rubric.excellent: Selects B and notes that the Cārvāka argument anticipates Hume's problem of induction: we can only verify connections we have actually observed, but inferences typically involve universal claims ('wherever there is smoke, there is fire — always, everywhere') that cannot be exhaustively verified by finite observation.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without connecting to the philosophical tradition of the problem.
rubric.weak: Selects A (psychological critique not present in Cārvāka), C (linguistic critique), or D (mystical alternative — directly contrary to Cārvāka's materialism).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D — projecting a mystical epistemology onto Cārvāka, which is explicitly a materialist, empiricist school that rejects mystical experience as a knowledge source.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Cārvāka section in exp-indian-lecture; ask the learner to reconstruct what Cārvāka says IS a valid source of knowledge and what it REJECTS, clarifying the school's strict empiricist and materialist commitments.

questions.prompt:
The Jain doctrine of anekāntavāda (many-sidedness) is sometimes confused with relativism. Which of the following BEST explains why it is NOT relativism?
A) Anekāntavāda says that some claims are simply false, not just partial
B) Anekāntavāda says that reality is complex and any single perspective is necessarily partial — but this is a claim about the limits of perspective, not that all claims are equally valid
C) Anekāntavāda is a religious doctrine rather than an epistemological one
D) Anekāntavāda only applies to metaphysical questions, not to practical ethics
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain that anekāntavāda uses the seven-fold predication as a rigorous logical framework — it is not 'anything goes' but a structured account of how perspective affects truth-claims

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that Jain anekāntavāda qualifies claims with syāt ('from a certain standpoint') to capture the multi-dimensional nature of reality — this is not relativism (which holds that all claims are equally valid) but a rigorous attempt to map the perspectival structure of knowledge.
rubric.adequate: Selects B with minimal elaboration.
rubric.weak: Selects A (anekāntavāda does not simply assert some claims are false), C or D.

errorPatterns.description: Learner confuses anekāntavāda with relativism — treating 'all perspectives are partial' as equivalent to 'all perspectives are equally valid' — reflecting the misconception that acknowledging perspectival limitation entails abandoning the capacity for better and worse claims.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Jainism section in exp-indian-lecture; ask the learner to identify how the seven-fold predication (saptabhaṅgī) is a structured logical framework, not an assertion of equivalence — distinguishing systematic perspectivism from anything-goes relativism.

questions.prompt: In 3–4 sentences, reconstruct the Buddhist argument from the Anattalakkhaṇa Sutta against the existence of a permanent self. Include the key premise, the test applied to the aggregates, and the conclusion.
successCriteria: States the key premise: if something were your self, you would have complete control over it
States the test: do you have complete control over form, sensation, perception, mental formations, or consciousness?
States the conclusion: since none of the five aggregates is under complete control, none is the self

rubric.excellent: States: Key premise — if X were your self, you would have complete control over X (you could dictate 'let it be thus or not thus'). Test — the Buddha applies this to each of the five aggregates: form (the body), sensation, perception, mental formations, and consciousness. Finding — we do not have complete control over any of these: they age, fall ill, change against our will. Conclusion — therefore none of the five aggregates is the permanent, controlling self (ātman). Notes (ideally) that the argument contains an unstated assumption: that selfhood requires complete control.
rubric.adequate: Reconstructs the premise and conclusion but omits the test applied to the aggregates, or names only one or two aggregates.
rubric.weak: Paraphrases 'there is no self' without reconstructing the argument structure.

errorPatterns.description: Learner describes the anattā doctrine as the claim that there is 'no soul and no reincarnation' — conflating the philosophical argument about the non-existence of a permanent controlling self with a folk understanding of Buddhism that equates anattā with total nihilism.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the anattā worked example (we-indian-anatta); ask the learner to distinguish between 'there is no permanent, controlling ātman' (what the argument establishes) and 'there is no causal continuity between mental states' (what the argument does NOT establish, and what dependent origination addresses).

questions.prompt:
Confucius's concept of zhengming (rectification of names) most precisely prescribes:
A) Standardizing vocabulary so that all citizens use the same terms
B) Ensuring that people actually fulfill the roles and obligations designated by their titles
C) Correcting factual errors in historical and administrative records
D) Defining philosophical terms precisely before arguing about them
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain why name-reality alignment is the foundation of social order in Confucian thought

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that for Confucius, social order depends on the alignment between roles (their names) and the actual conduct of those roles — when a ruler exploits rather than governs, a father neglects rather than nurtures, language and moral expectation lose their grip on reality. Zhengming is thus a moral and political doctrine, not a linguistic one.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without articulating the political dimension.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C, or D — treating zhengming as a linguistic or epistemic doctrine rather than a moral-political one.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D — treating Confucian concern for names as a matter of intellectual precision rather than moral-political alignment between role-titles and actual conduct, reflecting a misreading of Confucianism as primarily concerned with correct discourse rather than correct action.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Chinese context section and the Confucianism section in exp-chinese-lecture; ask the learner to identify what specific social disorders Confucius says arise from name-reality misalignment and why this is a political prescription.

questions.prompt:
Zhuangzi's Butterfly Dream illustrates which philosophical point?
A) Dreams reveal deeper truths about reality than waking experience
B) No single perspective, including the waking human perspective, is the definitively authoritative one
C) The self is identical to nature and should be aligned with its rhythms
D) We should not trust our senses because they can mislead us
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain that the dream shows perspective constitutes our sense of what is real, not that senses are unreliable or dreams are privileged

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains: the Butterfly Dream does not privilege dream experience over waking experience or vice versa — it demonstrates that each perspective is positionally constituted, and no perspective can step outside all positions to declare itself the authoritative one. This is Zhuangzi's perspectivism, not Cartesian skepticism about the senses.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without distinguishing Zhuangzian perspectivism from Cartesian sensory skepticism.
rubric.weak: Selects A (privileging dreams), C (misidentifying the point with Daoist nature-alignment rather than perspectivism), or D (generic sensory skepticism).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects C — conflating the Butterfly Dream's point about perspectivism with wu wei's point about aligning with nature, suggesting they have not distinguished Zhuangzi's different philosophical contributions.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Daoism section in exp-chinese-lecture; ask the learner to state what the Cook parable illustrates vs. what the Butterfly Dream illustrates — identifying two distinct philosophical points made by Zhuangzi.

questions.prompt:
Mozi argued for universal love (jian ai) on explicitly consequentialist grounds. Which of the following BEST states the critical empirical premise of his argument — the premise most vulnerable to challenge?
A) Most people are capable of universal love if they try hard enough
B) If everyone practiced universal love, major social harms would be eliminated
C) Graduated love (loving family more than strangers) is morally wrong
D) The goal of ethics is to maximize benefit and minimize harm for everyone
successCriteria: Selects B

Can explain that B is an empirical claim about social dynamics that Mozi does not adequately defend — large-scale harms may arise from institutional incentives, not just individual failures of equal concern

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that most large-scale social harms (structural poverty, institutional discrimination) are produced by complex institutional incentives and collective action problems, not by individual failures of equal concern — even universally caring people might make different judgments about how to serve all interests, producing conflict without malice.
rubric.adequate: Selects B and identifies it as empirical without elaborating on what specifically challenges it.
rubric.weak: Selects A (psychological possibility premise), C (the moral claim), or D (the consequentialist axiom — which Mozi would embrace and not consider critically).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D — treating Mozi's consequentialist axiom as the most contestable claim rather than the empirical premise about social outcomes, showing a failure to distinguish which premises are more vs. less empirically vulnerable.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Mohist worked example (we-chinese-mohist) and the critique in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to identify specifically why B (not D) is the premise that does the most work and is most vulnerable to the Legalist challenge.

questions.prompt:
According to Confucius in the Analects, ritual (li) divorced from ren (humaneness) is:
A) Still valuable because it maintains social order regardless of inner state
B) Empty — without genuine humaneness, ritual performance is hollow and does not accomplish its purpose
C) More reliable than ren-based action because it is more predictable
D) Acceptable as a starting point for developing genuine virtue
successCriteria: Selects B
Cites Analects 3.3: 'If a man has no humaneness, what can ritual do for him?'

rubric.excellent: Selects B and cites or paraphrases Analects 3.3 ('If a man has no humaneness, what can ritual do for him?'), explaining that for Confucius, li is the training ground for ren — it cultivates genuine care through practice. Ritual without genuine ren is like playing scales without caring about music: technically proficient but missing the point.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without citing the Analects passage or explaining the li-ren relationship.
rubric.weak: Selects A (Legalist-style position that social order matters regardless of inner state — plausible but not Confucian), C, or D.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — treating Confucianism as a doctrine of social conformity where external ritual behavior is sufficient regardless of inner state, reproducing exactly the misconception the section addresses.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the chinese-misconception content block and Analects 3.3; ask the learner to explain in their own words what Confucius means by saying ritual without humaneness is empty — and what distinguishes genuine li from mere social conformism.

questions.prompt: Legalism and Confucianism both aim at a well-functioning society. In 3–4 sentences, explain their core disagreement about HOW to achieve this — specifically, what each school thinks is the reliable foundation for social order.
successCriteria: States the Confucian foundation: virtuous rulers and people who have cultivated ren and li; moral example cascades through society
States the Legalist foundation: well-designed laws (fa), administrative structures, and incentives that make good behavior rational regardless of individual virtue
Identifies the specific disagreement: whether virtue or institutional incentives is the more reliable foundation

rubric.excellent: States: Confucianism holds that social order depends on virtuous rulers who cultivate ren and model li — moral example cascades through society (Analects 2.1: the virtuous ruler is like the North Star). Legalism holds that this is dangerously naive: most people, most of the time, are motivated by self-interest, not virtue. Relying on virtuous rulers is like relying on perfect weather. The Legalist prescription: design laws (fa), administrative structures (shu), and power dynamics (shi) so that even a mediocre ruler can govern effectively — because institutions create the right incentives regardless of individual character.
rubric.adequate: States the key distinction (virtue vs. institutions) but does not articulate why Legalists consider the Confucian approach naive or what specific institutional mechanisms they prescribe.
rubric.weak: States both schools want social order without identifying the substantive disagreement about its source.

errorPatterns.description: Learner describes Confucianism as 'rule following' and Legalism as 'strict rules' — missing the philosophical depth on both sides: the Confucian insight about the role of virtue and moral example vs. the Legalist insight about institutional incentive design.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the Confucianism and Legalism sections in exp-chinese-lecture; ask the learner to identify specifically what each school says about the RELIABILITY of virtue as a foundation for social order and why they reach different conclusions.

questions.prompt:
Which of the following BEST describes the error of over-assimilation in comparative philosophy?
A) Treating traditions as completely separate and incommensurable
B) Using the vocabulary of one tradition to interpret another
C) Treating surface similarities as indicating identical doctrines, ignoring deeper structural differences
D) Focusing only on the historical differences between traditions rather than philosophical content
successCriteria: Selects C
Can give a specific example — e.g., treating Stoic and Buddhist detachment as identical

rubric.excellent: Selects C and gives a specific example: e.g., treating Stoic and Buddhist detachment as identical ignores that the Stoic preserves the rational self while the Buddhist dissolves it — same word, opposite metaphysical foundations.
rubric.adequate: Selects C without a specific example.
rubric.weak: Selects A (this is over-differentiation, not over-assimilation), B, or D.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — confusing the two comparative errors, identifying over-differentiation (treating traditions as incommensurable) when asked about over-assimilation.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the comp-method content block in exp-comparative-lecture; ask the learner to state one clear example of each error using specific concepts from the course.

questions.prompt:
The Stoic says: 'Only virtue is genuinely good; external things are preferred indifferents.' The Buddhist says: 'Nothing in the conditioned world is permanently satisfying; craving is the source of suffering.' A student claims these are the same idea. What is the MOST important structural difference the student is missing?
A) The Stoic framework is Western and the Buddhist framework is Eastern — they are simply using different cultural language for the same insight
B) The Stoic preserves a permanent rational self as the agent of virtue; the Buddhist dissolves the very self as the source of the most fundamental craving
C) The Stoic is concerned with politics and community; the Buddhist is concerned only with individual liberation
D) The Stoic argues for virtue based on reason; the Buddhist argues for liberation based on meditation
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain that this difference in self-doctrine means the two practices, pursued to their conclusions, lead in opposite directions

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains: the Stoic's entire project is to cultivate and express the rational self — a stable, sovereign agent of virtue. The Buddhist's entire project is to see through the illusion of such an agent. A Stoic practicing eupatheiai is strengthening the rational self; a Buddhist practicing non-attachment is dissolving self-identification. Same surface prescription (do not cling), opposite ultimate goals.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without explaining why this difference matters for the two practices.
rubric.weak: Selects A (the geographic/cultural explanation dismisses the philosophical content), C (overgeneralizes the communal/individual distinction), or D (focuses on method rather than the self-doctrine at stake).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — treating the Stoic-Buddhist difference as merely cultural/linguistic rather than substantive philosophical divergence rooted in incompatible self-doctrines.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the comp-testcase content block in exp-comparative-lecture; ask the learner to trace what each tradition says about the existence of a permanent self and why that difference makes the two practices lead in opposite directions when pursued to their logical conclusions.

questions.prompt: A student argues: 'Aristotle and Confucianism are basically the same — both say virtue and community are essential for the good life.' Using the shared-problem method, identify ONE genuine agreement and ONE structural difference between these two traditions on this question.
successCriteria: States a genuine agreement (both traditions hold that living well requires both virtue and community; neither accepts pure individualism or purely external compliance)

States a structural difference (e.g., Aristotle's community enables individual flourishing; Confucianism's relationships constitute the person — the individual does not exist prior to relationships. OR: Aristotle's virtues are stable individual dispositions; Confucian virtue is expressed through role-fulfillment in specific relational contexts)

rubric.excellent: Genuine agreement: both traditions hold that the good life requires cultivated virtue AND active participation in a community — neither pure introspection nor external compliance is sufficient. Structural difference: Aristotle's political community enables and is the context for an individual's flourishing — the individual has a prior identity that the community serves. Confucianism's relationships are constitutive of the person — there is no 'you' prior to your relational identity as parent, child, friend, official. This means Confucian ethics is irreducibly social in a way Aristotelian ethics is not.
rubric.adequate: States a genuine agreement and a plausible difference but does not articulate the constitutive vs. enabling distinction precisely.
rubric.weak: States only similarities ('both care about virtue') without identifying a structural difference, or states only differences ('one is Chinese and one is Greek') without philosophical content.

errorPatterns.description: Learner claims Aristotle and Confucianism have nothing in common because they developed in different cultures — treating cultural difference as philosophical incommensurability.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the comp-virtue content block in exp-comparative-lecture; ask the learner to identify three specific philosophical claims both traditions make about virtue and community before identifying where they structurally diverge.

questions.prompt: The Jain doctrine of anekāntavāda (many-sidedness) and Zhuangzi's perspectivism both hold that no single perspective is fully adequate. In 3–4 sentences, identify ONE genuine similarity and ONE significant difference between how these two traditions develop this insight.
successCriteria: States a genuine similarity (both hold that perspectives are inherently partial; dogmatic commitment to any single framework is epistemic failure)

States a significant difference (Jainism develops a formal logical apparatus — the seven-fold predication — to rigorously map perspectival claims; Zhuangzi uses paradox, parable, and humor rather than formal logic)

rubric.excellent: Genuine similarity: both traditions hold that all cognitive perspectives are partial — any single claim captures only some aspects of a complex reality, and dogmatic commitment to a single framework constitutes epistemic failure. Significant difference: Jainism responds to this insight by developing a formal logical framework (the seven-fold predication: saptabhaṅgī) that systematically maps how a claim can be true, false, both, indescribable, or combinations of these from different standpoints. Zhuangzi responds to the same insight through paradox, parable (the Butterfly Dream, the Cook and the Ox), and humor — showing perspectivism in action rather than systematizing it.
rubric.adequate: States the similarity and one dimension of the difference but does not identify the specific formal apparatus of Jainism.
rubric.weak: States only that both traditions value humility without articulating the perspectivism insight or the difference in how each develops it.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats both anekāntavāda and Zhuangzian perspectivism as forms of relativism — concluding that both traditions hold all claims are equally valid — missing the philosophical precision that perspectivism is about the limits of perspective, not the equivalence of all claims.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the comp-epistemology content block in exp-comparative-lecture; ask the learner to identify what both Jain and Daoist perspectivism still require for making better vs. worse claims — identifying the contrast with pure relativism.

assessments.title: Section 6 Quiz: Philosophical Debate and Critique

questions.prompt:
Which of the following BEST describes the principle of steelmanning?
A) Defending your own position as strongly as possible before acknowledging objections
B) Constructing the strongest possible version of your opponent's argument before critiquing it
C) Agreeing with your opponent's strongest points before explaining why you still disagree
D) Using vivid analogies and examples to make your position more persuasive
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain why steelmanning is required for a critique to actually establish something

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that if you critique a weak version of an opposing position (strawmanning), you have not shown anything about the real position — you have only refuted a caricature. Steelmanning ensures that your critique, if successful, actually applies to the position as its best proponents would defend it.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without explaining why it is required for the critique to be meaningful.
rubric.weak: Selects A (self-defense), C (partial acknowledgment without the steelmanning requirement), or D (rhetoric).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects A — treating philosophical debate as primarily about defending one's own position rather than engaging rigorously with the opposing position, reflecting the win-mindset misconception.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the debate-whatitis content block in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to explain what the difference is between steelmanning your opponent and merely acknowledging that they have a point — identifying the structural requirement.

questions.prompt:
In the worked debate in Section 6, the Stoic responds to the Aristotelian objection that 'the sage happy under torture is unrealistic.' Which step of the four-step response structure does the Stoic use when saying: 'The objection is right that no ordinary human fully achieves sagehood under extreme duress'?
A) Identify the specific claim the objection challenges
B) Acknowledge what is right in the objection
C) Respond to that specific challenge
D) Reassert the overall thesis
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain why acknowledging what is right in an objection is philosophically important rather than a concession of defeat

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains that acknowledging what is right demonstrates that you have understood the objection (building credibility) and prevents the response from being mere reassertion. Acknowledging a valid point in an objection while maintaining your thesis is philosophically stronger than pretending the objection has no force.
rubric.adequate: Selects B without explaining why acknowledgment is philosophically important.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C, or D — confusing which step of the four-part structure the quoted phrase exemplifies.

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects D (reassertion) — treating the acknowledgment as the Stoic's final move rather than the first step of a structured response, suggesting they conflate acknowledging a valid point with conceding the entire position.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the debate-respondtoobjections content block in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to identify all four steps of the Stoic response to the Aristotelian critique and label each one — showing the full structure of a rigorous response.

questions.prompt: A student writes: 'Aristotle's virtue ethics is wrong because he excluded women and slaves from his account of flourishing. This shows his ethics is just ancient bias, not genuine philosophy.' Using the concepts from Section 6, identify what is philosophically weak about this critique and what a stronger version of it would look like.
successCriteria: Identifies that the critique as stated attacks a weak version of Aristotle (the historical exclusions) without engaging with the philosophical core of the virtue ethics framework
Explains that a stronger critique would steelman the best version of Aristotelian virtue ethics and then show that the core claims — not just the exclusions — contain problematic assumptions

Describes what a steelmanned critique would look like: e.g., that virtue ethics in its Aristotelian form presupposes stable social conditions and a community within which virtues are defined — this may be a genuine structural limit, not just a historical accident

rubric.excellent: Identifies the weakness: the critique attacks the historical exclusions (which Aristotle himself does not argue for philosophically — they are assumptions) rather than the philosophical core of virtue ethics. A stronger critique would steelman the best version of Aristotelian virtue ethics and show that either: (1) the core claims (habituation, practical wisdom, the mean) depend on assumptions that have problematic implications even when the exclusions are removed; or (2) the framework requires a stable, well-functioning polis that is not available to marginalized people, making its prescriptions inapplicable in ways that are philosophically significant, not just historically contingent.
rubric.adequate: Identifies that the critique attacks the exclusions rather than the philosophical core, but does not articulate what a stronger steelmanned critique would look like.
rubric.weak: Defends Aristotle by saying the exclusions were normal for his time, or agrees with the student's critique without engaging with the steelmanning requirement.

errorPatterns.description: Learner defends Aristotle by attacking the student's motivation or historical context rather than engaging with the philosophical question of whether the critique targets the right thing — treating critique as an attack to be repelled rather than an engagement to be evaluated.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the debate-workedcritique content block in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to apply the four-step critique method (steelman, identify critical premise, challenge, distinguish what the critique establishes) to the student's critique of Aristotle.

questions.prompt: Construct a philosophical argument with: (a) a clear thesis about a question from ancient philosophy (your choice), (b) at least two explicit premises, (c) a conclusion, and (d) the strongest objection to your argument with your response using the four-step structure.
successCriteria: States a clear, specific thesis (not a vague claim)
States at least two explicit premises that support the thesis
States a conclusion that follows from the premises
Identifies the strongest objection (steelmanned) and responds using all four steps: acknowledge, identify, respond, reassert

rubric.excellent: Thesis is clear and specific. Premises are distinct and individually defensible. Conclusion follows. Objection is steelmanned — the strongest version, not a weak caricature. Response: acknowledges what is right in the objection, identifies which specific premise or inference it challenges, responds to that challenge specifically, and reasserts the thesis in light of the exchange.
rubric.adequate: Thesis and premises are present but one premise is vague or the objection is slightly strawmanned; the four-step response is mostly present but one step is thin.
rubric.weak: Thesis is a truism or too vague to argue for; premises are not distinguishable from the thesis; objection is a strawman; response is mere reassertion or total capitulation.

errorPatterns.description: Learner's response to the objection consists of 'You are right, my position has problems' without rebuilding — treating acknowledgment of a valid point as capitulation of the entire thesis.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the debate-respondtoobjections worked example in exp-debate-lecture; ask the learner to identify the difference between 'the objection shows my premise needs refinement' (not capitulation) and 'the objection shows my thesis is false' (potential capitulation) — and practice rebuilding from an acknowledged objection.

assessments.title: Section 7 Quiz: Living Philosophy — Application to Contemporary Life

questions.prompt:
Which of the following BEST describes the difference between wholesale adoption and selective adaptation when applying an ancient philosophical framework?
A) Wholesale adoption requires studying the tradition more deeply; selective adaptation is for beginners
B) Wholesale adoption means accepting all of a tradition's claims without examination; selective adaptation means choosing the most relevant elements, identifying their limits, and modifying or supplementing them where needed
C) Wholesale adoption is more philosophically rigorous because it avoids cherry-picking
D) Selective adaptation means combining two traditions randomly to get the answer you prefer
successCriteria: Selects B
Can explain why wholesale adoption is not philosophically rigorous and selective adaptation is not arbitrary

rubric.excellent: Selects B and explains: wholesale adoption exchanges one unexamined authority for another — it is not philosophical engagement. Selective adaptation is not arbitrary; it requires justifying your selections explicitly (this framework addresses this type of problem), identifying specific limits (this assumption does not hold in my context), and explaining your adaptations (therefore I modify it in this way, drawing on this other resource).
rubric.adequate: Selects B without distinguishing principled selective adaptation from arbitrary cherry-picking.
rubric.weak: Selects A, C (wholesale adoption is more rigorous), or D (random combination).

errorPatterns.description: Learner selects C — treating wholesale adoption as more philosophically rigorous than selective adaptation, reflecting the misconception that commitment to a complete system is a sign of philosophical seriousness.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the app-mistakes content block in exp-application-lecture; ask the learner to explain specifically what makes wholesale adoption philosophically problematic — and what distinguishes justified selective adaptation from arbitrary borrowing.

questions.prompt: A person is dealing with rage and grief after an unexpected job loss. Which ancient framework does the app-frameworkguide in Section 7 identify as MOST useful for this kind of situation, and what specific concept from that framework is most directly applicable?
successCriteria: Identifies Stoicism as most useful for situations involving loss of control, grief, or circumstances one cannot change
Identifies the dichotomy of control (Epictetus) as the most directly applicable concept
Can state what falls inside and outside the dichotomy in this specific scenario

rubric.excellent: Identifies Stoicism and the dichotomy of control (Epictetus): the job loss, other people's decisions, and economic conditions are 'not up to us' — outside the rational will. What is 'up to us': how we judge the loss (as catastrophic vs. as a difficult circumstance to navigate), what we pursue in response, how we maintain our values and relationships going forward. The Stoic framework helps clarify what the person has agency over rather than directing emotional energy at what they cannot control.
rubric.adequate: Identifies Stoicism and the dichotomy of control but does not apply them specifically to the job loss scenario.
rubric.weak: Identifies a different framework without justification, or names Stoicism without identifying the specific relevant concept.

errorPatterns.description: Learner says 'no ancient framework is useful for modern job loss — this is too contemporary a situation for ancient philosophy to address,' reflecting the obsolescence misconception.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the app-why content block and the chronic illness worked example (we-app-stoicism-illness) in exp-application-lecture; ask the learner to identify what structural similarity connects the job loss situation to the conditions for which Stoic philosophy was originally developed.

questions.prompt: The Section 7 lecture notes two specific limits of applying classical Buddhist frameworks to contemporary life. Name one of these limits and explain what historical assumption it reflects.
successCriteria: States one of the two limits accurately: (1) classical Buddhist ethics was developed in a monastic context presupposing near-total renunciation of household life; or (2) the anattā doctrine seems to undermine long-term planning and commitment, addressed only by later Buddhist accounts of 'conventional self'

Explains the historical assumption: (1) the framework was designed for monks, not householders with family, career, and civic obligations; or (2) the earliest formulations did not fully address the tension between ultimate no-self and the practical continuity required for life in the world

rubric.excellent: States: Buddhist ethics was developed in a monastic context — it often presupposes total or near-total renunciation of household life. This reflects the historical assumption that the path to liberation requires withdrawal from ordinary social and family obligations. Adapting it to the life of a person with family, career, and civic obligations requires significant work. OR: The anattā doctrine seems to undermine long-term planning and commitment — if there is truly no persistent self, who is responsible for past actions? This tension was only addressed by later Buddhist traditions that developed accounts of 'conventional self' (real enough for practical purposes) alongside 'ultimate no-self.'
rubric.adequate: States one limit accurately but does not articulate the specific historical assumption it reflects.
rubric.weak: States a vague limit ('it's too spiritual for modern life') without specificity.

errorPatterns.description: Learner states 'Buddhism is obsolete because it's from ancient India' — treating the limit as temporal rather than identifying the specific structural assumption (monastic context, anattā-commitment tension) that creates the limit.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the app-limits content block in exp-application-lecture; ask the learner to distinguish between 'this framework is old' (not a philosophical limit) and 'this framework assumes X which doesn't hold in my context' (a genuine philosophical limit).

questions.prompt: Apply the five-feature application method to the following situation: A manager in a large organization discovers that a colleague they respect has been systematically taking credit for their team's work. They feel angry and betrayed, and must decide how to respond — whether to confront the colleague directly, escalate to leadership, do nothing, or something else. Select one or two relevant frameworks, apply them faithfully, identify at least one limit, and describe a justified selective adaptation.
successCriteria: Selects a framework relevant to this type of situation (injustice, competing obligations, decision under uncertainty) and justifies the selection
Applies the framework specifically — not a vague paraphrase but actual doctrines applied to the concrete situation
Identifies at least one specific limit the framework has for this situation
Describes a selective adaptation that addresses the limit, with justification

rubric.excellent: Framework selection: Aristotle's virtue ethics is relevant (what would a person of practical wisdom and the relevant virtues — courage, justice, honesty — do?). Application: practical wisdom (phronesis) means attending to the particulars: who is the colleague, what is the relationship, what are the stakes for the team, what is the organizational context? The virtuous mean for honesty requires addressing the injustice (deficiency: doing nothing, complicity with the wrong); the virtuous mean for courage requires confronting it appropriately (excess: scorched-earth escalation that damages relationships unnecessarily). Limit: Aristotle's framework was developed for a small face-to-face community (the polis) — applying it to a large modern organization with power asymmetries and institutional procedures requires translation. Selective adaptation: supplement with Confucian awareness of role-based relationships (the manager-colleague relationship has specific obligations) and Stoic attention to what is within vs. outside control (the colleague's response to confrontation is not within control; choosing to speak honestly is).
rubric.adequate: Selects a relevant framework, applies it with some specificity, identifies a plausible limit, and describes a selective adaptation — but one element is thin or vague.
rubric.weak: Selects a framework without justification, or applies it only vaguely, or does not identify a genuine limit, or treats the 'adaptation' as simply saying 'be reasonable.'

errorPatterns.description: Learner applies Stoicism by saying 'just accept the situation and focus on what you can control' — using the framework as a formula that eliminates the need for practical judgment rather than as a tool that clarifies the situation and supports deliberation.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the app-workedexample content block (chronic illness application) in exp-application-lecture; ask the learner to identify Step 4 (limits) in that example and apply the same step to their Stoic application — identifying specifically where 'focus on what you can control' does not resolve the situation and what supplementation is needed.

glossary.definition: The systematic, self-critical examination of foundational assumptions about reality, knowledge, and value, using argument and reason as primary tools.
Karl Jaspers' term for the period ~800–200 BCE during which parallel philosophical traditions emerged across Greece, India, and China, driven by literacy, pluralism, and crisis.
A text produced by the original thinker or school being studied, as opposed to a secondary source that describes or interprets that thinker.
In the technical philosophical sense: a set of premises offered as reasons to accept a conclusion.
The claims in an argument that are offered as reasons or evidence for the conclusion.
The claim an argument is trying to establish, supported by its premises.

Greek: variously 'word,' 'reason,' 'rational principle,' or 'argument.' Used differently by Heraclitus (rational principle governing flux), Stoics (cosmic rational order shared by all humans), and John's Gospel. No single English equivalent.
Sanskrit: 'cosmic order,' 'duty,' 'the Buddha's teaching,' or 'element of experience' depending on school and context. Translating it as simply 'law' or 'duty' collapses its range.

Chinese: 'The Way' — the underlying principle and process of all things in Daoist philosophy. Inherently beyond full verbal description; the Tao Te Ching opens by asserting that the Dao that can be spoken is not the enduring Dao.
Socratic cross-examination: a method of questioning in which Socrates asks someone to define a concept (justice, courage, piety) and then exposes contradictions in their answers, leading to aporia.
A state of genuine puzzlement or perplexity reached through elenchus, in which false certainty is cleared away and real inquiry can begin.

Plato's doctrine that for every general category (Beauty, Justice, Good, Circle), there is a perfect, eternal, non-physical pattern (Form) of which all physical instances are imperfect copies. The Forms are more real than physical things.
Aristotle's doctrine that every natural substance is a combination of matter (hyle) and form (morphe). Forms exist in particular things, not in a separate realm.
Aristotle's term for the purpose or final cause of a natural thing — the characteristic function that defines what it is. An eye's telos is to see; a human's telos is eudaimonia.

Usually translated 'happiness' or 'flourishing.' For Aristotle: rational activity in accordance with virtue over a complete life, requiring both character and sufficient external goods. For Stoics: the state of a virtuous person, achievable regardless of external circumstances.
Greek: virtue or excellence — a stable disposition (hexis) to feel and act appropriately in the relevant domain. Acquired through habituation.
Aristotle's term for a settled disposition or habit — the psychological structure of virtue. Virtues are hexeis: stable tendencies to respond well to situations.

Epictetus's Stoic division of everything into what is 'up to us' (our own judgments, desires, impulses, and aversions) and what is 'not up to us' (our body, wealth, reputation, how others treat us). Freedom and eudaimonia come from focusing entirely on the first category.

Stoic: healthy, appropriate emotions that arise from correct beliefs about what is genuinely valuable — contrasted with pathological passions arising from false beliefs. Examples: joy (not pleasure), caution (not fear), wish (not desire). The Stoic goal is emotional accuracy, not emotional absence.

Tranquility or freedom from mental disturbance — the highest good according to both Epicureans (achieved through modest pleasures and freedom from unnecessary desires) and Pyrrhonist Skeptics (achieved through suspension of judgment).
Pyrrhonist Skepticism: suspension of judgment — the appropriate response to the equal weight of opposing arguments (isostheneia). The Skeptics claimed suspension of judgment produces ataraxia.
Epicurus: stable, undisturbed contentment — the highest form of pleasure, more reliable and more valuable than kinetic (active) pleasures.

Epicurus's 'four-part cure': (1) Do not fear the gods — they do not intervene. (2) Do not fear death — when I am, death is not; when death is, I am not. (3) What is good is easy to get — natural, necessary pleasures. (4) What is terrible is easy to endure.
Sanskrit: 'orthodox' — schools of Indian philosophy that accept the authority of the Vedas. The six āstika schools are Nyāya, Vaiśeṣika, Sāṃkhya, Yoga, Mīmāṃsā, and Vedānta.
Sanskrit: 'heterodox' — schools of Indian philosophy that reject Vedic authority. The major nāstika schools are Buddhism, Jainism, and Cārvāka.
In Upanishadic and Vedāntic philosophy: the ultimate, infinite, formless ground of all being. In Advaita Vedānta, identical to the individual self (ātman).
Sanskrit: the individual self or soul. In Advaita Vedānta, identical to Brahman. The Buddha's anattā doctrine directly denies the existence of a permanent ātman.
Often translated 'illusion,' but more precisely a veiling or misapprehension of the underlying unity of Brahman. The appearance of a separate, multiple world is māyā.
Liberation — freedom from the cycle of rebirth (saṃsāra) and suffering. Each school defines liberation differently depending on its account of the self and of what we are liberated from.
In Sāṃkhya philosophy: pure, passive consciousness — the witness. Multiple individual Puruṣas exist, each distinct. Contrasted with Prakṛti (material nature).
In Sāṃkhya philosophy: the single material principle from which all physical and psychological reality evolves. Composed of three guṇas. Unconscious and active — contrasted with passive Puruṣa.
The three qualities of Prakṛti in Sāṃkhya: sattva (clarity/goodness), rajas (activity/passion), and tamas (inertia/darkness). All physical and psychological phenomena are combinations of these three.
Sanskrit: a valid source of knowledge. Nyāya identifies four: pratyakṣa (perception), anumāna (inference), upamāna (comparison/analogy), and śabda (reliable testimony).

Sanskrit: inference — one of the four Nyāya pramāṇas. Structured as a five-member argument (pañcāvayava) whose conclusion is grounded through the vyāpti (pervasion) relationship between sign and signified.

Sanskrit: pervasion — the universal connection between a sign (hetu) and its signified (sādhya) required for valid Nyāya inference. The connection between smoke and fire is an example of vyāpti grounded by repeated observation.

Sanskrit/Pali: suffering, unsatisfactoriness, or fundamental dis-ease — the first of the Four Noble Truths. All conditioned existence is characterized by dukkha because of impermanence and the absence of a permanent satisfying self.
Pali: impermanence — the Buddhist claim that all conditioned phenomena are transient. Nothing lasts. The second of the Three Marks of Existence.
Pali: non-self — the Buddhist denial of a permanent, unchanging self (ātman). What we call 'I' is a collection of five aggregates (khandhas) with no fixed core. One of the Three Marks of Existence.
Pali: the five aggregates that together constitute what we call a 'person' — form (body), sensation, perception, mental formations, and consciousness. None of the five is the permanent self.

Sanskrit: dependent origination — the Buddhist metaphysical principle that everything arises in dependence on conditions. 'When this is, that is; when this ceases, that ceases.' Nothing has svabhāva (own-nature); everything is relational.

'Blowing out' — the cessation of craving and suffering; the Buddhist liberation goal. Not exactly happiness in the Greek sense (which requires a self experiencing positively), but complete peaceful non-clinging.
In Jainism: a soul — the living, conscious principle that is originally pure, omniscient, and blissful but has become bound by karma. Liberation (moksha) is freeing the jīva from all karmic matter.
Non-violence — the ethical core of Jainism, extended to all living beings possessing a jīva (including insects, plants, and microorganisms). Also an important principle in Buddhism and Hinduism.
